title: Do Not Resolve to Act Against the Assembly's Dignity!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, figures, quoted reports, and paragraph order. The Tamil reading copy below restores the source-printed opening salutation and the scan-confirmed readings `110வது விதி` and `20ம் தேதி`, and corrects clear OCR character and word-boundary errors. The canonical source document remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: The Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly began its sitting on the 10th and has continued for several days. Because this government has not provided suitable seating for me in view of my health, I do not attend the Assembly. Yet I follow its proceedings through newspapers and television. The procedure remains the same as always: the Chief Minister reads every department's announcement under Rule 110; walkouts or expulsions occur; the opposition is denied an opportunity to speak; if an opposition member tries to speak about the ruling party or Chief Minister, certain ministers immediately interrupt and silence them; ministers answering debates on their departments' grants recite epics of praise for the Chief Minister; and they attack me as harshly as they possibly can.

The Industries Minister's first accusation against us was that the DMK leader and member Stalin were spreading a false claim that investors had left Tamil Nadu for Karnataka without identifying the factories concerned. Yet in the same reply, the minister admitted: "At a meeting conducted in Coimbatore by the Karnataka Chief Minister and officials, everyone attending was asked to complete an expression-of-interest form, and some did so." Does his own admission not establish that such a meeting took place in Coimbatore and that Karnataka's Chief Minister came there to invite Tamil Nadu's industrial investors?

The minister also said: "Andhra Pradesh invites them by offering inexpensive land and tax concessions. Whenever the Andhra Chief Minister passes through Chennai, he calls our industrialists and offers them low-cost land." Does this not itself reveal Tamil Nadu's condition? When another State's Chief Minister comes into a State governed by them, conducts a meeting and invites its industrial investors away, they should feel ashamed. What purpose is served by lunging at us?

The charge voiced by the Industries Minister was not truly his own. It was what Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said at an election campaign meeting in Coimbatore on 1 April. I answered that speech in detail in *Murasoli* on 3 April 2014. Yet the departmental minister repeated the same argument in the Assembly.

There are so many States in India. If Karnataka's Chief Minister comes to a Tamil Nadu city, gathers Tamil Nadu industrialists and asks them to establish industries in Karnataka, even an ordinary industrialist can understand what that means.

We did not invent the claim that Karnataka's Chief Minister held a meeting in Coimbatore and invited Tamil Nadu investors. *Dinamalar* is not a DMK publication; it severely criticises the DMK. On 22 January, it carried a report headlined: "Tamil Nadu Pushes Away; Karnataka Draws In: Investment of 12,000 Crore Rupees Slips from Our Hands."

The report said: "Investments worth 12,000 crore rupees from industrialists in the Kongu region are set to go to Karnataka. Tamil Nadu's deteriorating investment climate is said to be the principal cause. If this continues, industries in other parts of Tamil Nadu may also relocate to other States. An industrial zone is being established over 1,400 acres in Chamarajanagar district on the Tamil Nadu-Karnataka border. To attract investors, a Chamarajanagar investors' conference was held in Coimbatore on the 20th. Karnataka Chief Minister Siddaramaiah invited Tamil Nadu industrialists, saying, 'Come and start industries in Karnataka; we will provide every facility.' As a result, about 200 industrialists operating in Coimbatore, Tiruppur, Erode, Salem and other parts of the Kongu region have come forward to invest 12,000 crore rupees in Chamarajanagar."
This is the report we cited. The Chief Minister and departmental minister should rebut *Dinamalar*, not become angry with us.

After the conference, Vijayakumar, Assistant Secretary of the Federation of Karnataka Chambers of Commerce and Industry, said: "The Coimbatore conference was a tremendous success. It is deeply gratifying that, in a single day, investors came forward to commit more than 12,000 crore rupees to the Chamarajanagar industrial zone." That too appeared in the newspaper at the time.

Was it or was it not reported specifically that a Tiruppur export company agreed to invest 2,000 crore rupees and a woman entrepreneur from Coimbatore expressed interest in investing 1,000 crore rupees? Why did the minister not answer then? What does it mean to remain silent at the time and now storm into the Assembly demanding the names of factories and investment amounts?

On 24 January 2014, *Dinamalar* published a half-page report based on interviews about why investment was leaving Tamil Nadu. Its headline alone explains the situation: "Bribery, Power Cuts and Land Prices Torment Tamil Nadu: The Mystery Behind Western District Investments Moving to Karnataka!" Is it right to ignore such reports and become angry with the opposition?
How long has it been since the headline "Bribery Torments Tamil Nadu" appeared? Should the Tamil Nadu government not have answered it?

At Coimbatore, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said that during her three years in office, memoranda of understanding worth 31,706 crore rupees had been signed with 33 Indian and multinational companies. Can she state how many of those 33 companies have actually started operations in Tamil Nadu? MoUs may be signed with any number of companies, but they yield results only when industries actually begin functioning.

At the same election meeting, she herself said that of the 31,706 crore rupees covered by 33 MoUs, only 10,660 crore rupees in investment had been received. Yet the Industries Minister told the Assembly that 68 projects had attracted a total of 46,602.72 crore rupees over three years. Is the Chief Minister's figure true, or is the minister's Assembly figure true?
During the previous Assembly session, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa spoke at length about these MoUs, and I answered her then. The minister has merely repeated the same claims now.

In the reply she read during that session, Jayalalithaa said: "During the previous minority DMK government, 54 industrial enterprises were said to have committed 66,664 crore rupees through MoUs and Government Orders. Of these, twelve projects worth 15,982 crore rupees were never started."

Does this not mean that Jayalalithaa herself admitted in the Assembly that 42 projects worth 50,682 crore rupees were started under the Kazhagam government? Is that not evidence of industrial growth during DMK rule?

She also said that from May 2011 until now, MoUs worth 26,625 crore rupees had been signed with seventeen major Indian and multinational companies, potentially creating jobs for 146,000 people, but that only 10,660 crore rupees in investment had arrived and 10,022 direct jobs had been created. The MoUs covered 26,625 crore rupees, but actual investment was only 10,660 crore, merely forty per cent. The Chief Minister herself admitted that agreements were projected to create 146,000 jobs but investment sufficient for only 10,022 jobs had materialised.

How, then, could the Industries Minister claim yesterday that Puratchi Thalaivi Amma had created employment for nearly 162,667 people? Is that not contrary to the facts? May false information be given in the Assembly? Is this the sign of industrial growth?

In a special interview published on 4 February 2014, then Union Minister Sudarsana Natchiappan said: "The Union Industries Ministry has brought a massive industrial-development project to Tamil Nadu. The Union government will lend up to ninety per cent of the required investment. The Tamil Nadu government has shown no interest in implementing it." I asked then what Jayalalithaa would say to this criticism of her government's lack of concern for industrial growth. Did either she or this minister answer?

Under the DMK government, 493,000 women worked in factories. Under the present AIADMK government, the number is 378,000. Thus 115,000 women have lost employment. Is this the sign of industrial growth?

The minister also spoke at length about cement prices, claiming that prices rose during my government. My question was how the Kazhagam government acted when prices rose, and why this government has not acted now. We summoned the owners of cement-producing companies and asked them to reduce prices. They agreed to cut the price by twenty rupees a bag, and it was reported at the time.

We also ordered the government to procure cement and sell it at a subsidised price of 200 rupees per bag. Through the Tamil Nadu Civil Supplies Corporation, 48,496 metric tonnes were sold at reduced prices up to 31 March 2008; 563,955 metric tonnes in 2008-09; and 979,330 metric tonnes in 2009-10. Because the government itself competed with private firms and sold at lower prices, open-market cement prices also fell substantially.

Has the AIADMK government taken any such action during the past three years? Has it summoned even one cement producer for talks on reducing prices? According to *The Hindu* of 19 June 2014, a bag costing 285 rupees on 31 May had risen to 350 rupees by 17 June. According to *Dinamalar* of 21 June 2014, a bag costing 310 rupees the previous week had risen to 340 rupees. What answer will the minister who told the Assembly that cement prices rose only under Karunanidhi give to these reports?

The Industries Minister also discussed cement production. The Tamil Nadu government operates plants at Alangulam and Ariyalur. Under the DMK government in 2010-11, Alangulam produced 203,040 tonnes. Under the AIADMK in 2011-12, it produced 134,640 tonnes, a decline of 68,400 tonnes.

At the government cement plant in Ariyalur, production under the Kazhagam government was 490,035 tonnes. Under the AIADMK it was 388,055 tonnes, a decline of 101,980 tonnes. Is this the sign that industrial growth has flourished in Tamil Nadu?

Subramanian Swamy recently said of Tamil Nadu's industrial development: "There is neither industrial prosperity nor economic growth. I learnt that Karnataka Chief Minister Siddaramaiah has drawn the industrialists here to his State. Why did the Tamil Nadu government make no effort to retain the 12,000 crore rupees in industrial investment that moved from Tamil Nadu to Karnataka? Severe power shortages and non-cooperation by government officials exist at every level, so no one from other States is willing to invest in Tamil Nadu. The expectation that Tamil Nadu would become India's leading State in information technology has changed. The reason is the State government's poor administration."

Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, what purpose is served by becoming angry with us? Is it wrong for me to gather these details and issue a statement? Sheltered by the Assembly, should the minister mock me there as the "Hero of Statements" because he lacks the ability to answer my questions?
Chief Minister, do not resolve to continue conducting the Assembly in this manner and destroy the dignity of the Tamil Nadu Legislature!
With affection,
M. K.
17 July 2014
